About: It is about a German race game.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the later game Sorry!
It is associated with the Indian game Pachisi
It is associated with the Colombian game Parqués
It is associated with the American games Parcheesi, Aggravation, and Trouble
It is associated with the French game Jeu des petits chevaux
It is associated with the Chinese Aeroplane chess
It is associated with the English game Ludo.
ConsistsOf: It consists of four game pieces per player.
It consists of a circle of fields.
It consists of a cross position for rows.
It consists of a workshop in Munich-Giesing.
It consists of painted wooden pegs.
It consists of plastic colored pegs.
DevelopedBy: It was developed by Josef Friedrich Schmidt.
DevelopedIn: It was developed in a workshop in Munich-Giesing.
FoundedBy: It was founded by Josef Friedrich Schmidt.
HasA: It has a pattern for six players.
It has a special 55 cent stamp.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by a peg being sent back to the "out" field when another peg lands on it.
It is characterized by being a cross and circle game.
It is characterized by the circle being collapsed onto the cross.
It is characterized by players bringing their game pieces into the player's "home" row.
It is characterized by game pieces moving in a clockwise direction.
It is characterized by pieces being able to jump over other pieces.
It is characterized by pieces being able to throw out pieces from other players.
It is characterized by a player not being able to throw out his own pieces.
It is characterized by two pegs not being able to reside on the same circle.
It is characterized by requiring an exact roll of the dice to move inside the home area.
InfluencedBy: It was influenced by the English game Ludo.
InventedBy: It was invented by Josef Friedrich Schmidt.
InventedIn: It was invented in a workshop in Munich-Giesing.
IsA: It is a German race game.
It is a cross and circle game.
KnownAs: It is known as Man, Don't Get Angry.
KnownFor: It is known for being the most popular parlour game in Germany.
It is known for selling 70 million copies since its introduction in 1914.
It is known for being played in many European countries.
It is known for its rules having essentially not changed since 1914.
It is known for being popular during the First World War.
It is known for establishing itself in tournament form.
It is known for its 100th anniversary being celebrated with a special stamp.
It is known for setting the world record for simultaneous gameplay with 1692 people.
OccurredDuring: It occurred during the First World War.
OccurredIn: It occurred in 1907/1908.
It occurred in 1914.
It occurred in 1910.
It occurred in 1920.
It occurred in 1953.
It occurred in the early 20th century.
It occurred in the winter months of 1907/1908.
It occurred in West Germany.
It occurred in East Germany.
It occurred in other countries.
ProducedBy: It was produced in series from 1914 on.
PublishedAs: It was published as a card game with the title Mensch ärgere Dich nicht: Das Kartenspiel.
UsedAs: It is used as a promotional game.
It is used as a licensed edition.
It is used as a counterfeit.
It is used as a successor game.
It is used as a tournament game.
UsedFor: It is used for escaping boredom.
It is used for simultaneous gameplay.
UsedIn: It is used in many European countries.
It is used in East Germany.
It is used in West Germany.
It is used in other countries.
UsedTo: It is used to bring a piece into the game.
It is used to move a piece by the thrown number.
It is used to bring pieces into the player's home row.
It is used to move pieces clockwise over the board.
It is used to enter the circle at the start field.
It is used to advance pieces by the thrown number of dots on the die.
It is used to bring a piece into the game by placing one from the "out" area onto the "start" field.
It is used to move a piece if it cannot be brought into the game.
It is used to move inside of the home area.
It is used to get all of the pieces in home.
